en.extract:
Clapperton's spurfowl (Pternistis clappertoni) is a species of bird in the family Phasianidae.
It is found in Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Mali, Mauritania, Niger, Nigeria, Sudan, and Uganda.


Taxonomy
Clapperton's spurfowl was described in 1826 by John George Children and Nicholas Aylward Vigors in an appendix to the Narrative of Travels and Discoveries in Northern and Central Africa by the explorers Dixon Denham and Hugh Clapperton. Children and Vigors chose to honour Clapperton and coined the binomial name Francolinus clappertoni.  In their publication Children and Vigors did not specify where the specimen had been collected but the type locality was later designated as Borno in northeast Nigeria. The species is now placed in the genus Pternistis that was introduced by the German naturalist Johann Georg Wagler in 1832. A molecular phylogenetic study published in 2019 found that Clapperton's spurfowl is sister to Harwood's spurfowl.
Two subspecies are now recognised:

P. c. clappertoni (Children & Vigors, 1826) — Mali to south Sudan, east South Sudan, northeast Uganda and west Ethiopia
P. c. sharpii (Ogilvie-Grant, 1892) — north and central Ethiopia, Eritrea


Notes


References


External links

'''Clapperton's spurfowl''' ('''''Pternistis clappertoni''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Phasianidae]].
It is found in [[Cameroon]], [[Central African Republic]], [[Chad]], [[Eritrea]], [[Ethiopia]], [[Mali]], [[Mauritania]], [[Niger]], [[Nigeria]], [[Sudan]], and [[Uganda]].

==Taxonomy==
Clapperton's spurfowl was described in 1826 by [[John George Children]] and [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors]] in an appendix to the ''Narrative of Travels and Discoveries in Northern and Central Africa'' by the explorers [[Dixon Denham]] and [[Hugh Clapperton]]. Children and Vigors chose to honour Clapperton and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Francolinus clappertoni''.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Denham | first1=Dixon | author1-link=Dixon Denham | last2=Clapperton | first2=Hugh | author2-link=Hugh Clapperton | year=1826 | title=Narrative of Travels and Discoveries in Northern and Central Africa in the Years 1822, 1823 and 1824 | location=London | publisher=John Murray | at=Appendix XXI, p. 198 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DRDUEsmbn7kC&pg=RA1-PA198 }}</ref>  In their publication Children and Vigors did not specify where the specimen had been collected but the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] was later designated as [[Borno State|Borno]] in northeast Nigeria.<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1934 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=2 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=79 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482892 }}</ref> The species is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Pternistis]]'' that was introduced by the German naturalist [[Johann Georg Wagler]] in 1832.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Wagler | first=Johann Georg | author-link=Johann Georg Wagler | year=1832 | title=Neue Sippen und Gattungen der Säugthiere und Vögel | journal=Isis von Oken | volume=1832 | at=cols 1218–1235 [1229] | language=German, Latin | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/26455534 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Pheasants, partridges, francolins | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pheasants/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=8 February 2020 }}</ref> A [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2019 found that Clapperton's spurfowl is [[sister taxon|sister]] to [[Harwood's spurfowl]].<ref name=mandiwana>{{ cite journal | last1=Mandiwana-Neudani | first1=T.G. | last2=Little | first2=R.M. | last3=Crowe | first3=T.M. | last4=Bowie | first4=R.C.K. | year=2019 | title=Taxonomy, phylogeny and biogeography of African spurfowls Galliformes, Phasianidae, Phasianinae, Coturnicini: ''Pternistis'' spp. | journal=Ostrich | volume=90 | issue=2 | pages=145–172 | doi=10.2989/00306525.2019.1584925 | bibcode=2019Ostri..90..145M | s2cid=195417777 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are now recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''P. c. clappertoni'' <small>([[John George Children|Children]] & [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors]], 1826)</small> — Mali to south Sudan, east South Sudan, northeast Uganda and west Ethiopia{{efn|''P. c. clappertoni'' includes the proposed [[subspecies]] ''koenigseggi'', ''heuglini'', ''nigrosquamatus'' and ''gedgii''.<ref name=ioc/><ref name=mandiwana/>}}
* ''P. c. sharpii'' <small>([[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant|Ogilvie-Grant]], 1892)</small> — north and central Ethiopia, Eritrea
